Optimal average power for Tempered Glass cleaning without thermal damage
Optimal wavelength for Tempered Glass processing with good absorption
Optimal spot size for precision cleaning on Tempered Glass surface
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management
Optimal fluence threshold for contaminant removal without glass damage
Optimal pulse width for controlled ablation of contaminants
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

Optimal overlap ratio for complete coverage without excessive energy deposition
Optimal dwell time per cleaning spot

Typical range for 1064 nm fiber lasers in delicate substrate cleaning; starting at 100 W avoids thermal stress on tempered glass (high reflectivity ~8% at 1064 nm, low thermal conductivity ~1 W/m·K); max limited to prevent cracking (melting point ~1400°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and study in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on glass surface cleaning.

Slower speeds for heavy contamination (e.g., oils/residues) ensure complete removal; 1000 mm/s optimal for light contaminants on tempered glass to balance efficiency and minimize heat accumulation. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals and Journal of Cleaning Technology (2020) case studies.

Nanosecond regime preferred over picosecond/femtosecond for cost-effective cleaning with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm); 10 ns recommended for tempered glass to selectively ablate contaminants without inducing subsurface stress. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019) on pulsed laser interactions with glass.

30 kHz balances cleaning speed with reduced heat buildup (high rates increase accumulation, risking tempered glass fracture); typical for 1064 nm cleaning of transparent substrates. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2017) on repetition rate effects in laser ablation of dielectrics.

Fluence below tempered glass damage threshold (~4-6 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal; 1.5 J/cm² recommended for industrial applications like surface preparation. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2021) and SPIE proceedings on laser cleaning of optics.

200 μm spot for uniform coverage on large areas of tempered glass; smaller for fine details, larger for broad cleaning to reduce passes. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems.

Multi-pass (2 recommended) for complete removal of layered contaminants without exceeding thermal limits; single pass sufficient for light soils on tempered glass. Source: Industry practice in Laser Cleaning Handbook (2022) and case studies from glass manufacturing (e.g., Pilkington reports).

50% overlap ensures uniform cleaning without gaps or overexposure on reflective tempered glass surfaces; higher for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization studies in Surface and Coatings Technology (2016).
